## Overview

High-dimensional fixed effects solvers behave very differently depending on the structure of the fixed effects graph: some graphs converge in a handful of iterations, while others are notoriously slow to converge. To get a realistic picture of where different solvers in the open source ecosystem shine, and where each solver struggles, we lean on two complementary sources of benchmark data:

- [**Sergio Correia's HDFE benchmark suite**](https://www.scorreia.com/data/hdfe/index.html), the most extensive collection of public HDFE benchmark datasets we are aware of. It bundles real-world datasets (credit, soccer, enron, patents, …) that stress a wide range of graph topologies.
- A set of **synthetic DGPs** distributed with Correia's suite and with [**fixest**](https://lrberge.github.io/fixest/), which let us probe specific regimes (uniform vs. assortative matching) in a controlled way. 

::: {.callout}
For an explanation why different fixed effects / graph structures lead to differences in solver performance, please take a look at the [When Are Fixed Effects Estimations Difficult?](difficult-fixed-effects.md) vignette.
:::

We benchmark the following software packages and their solvers: 

- [**fixest**](https://lrberge.github.io/fixest/) (R), the reference HDFE implementation (and the package after which PyFixest is modeled), uses a MAP-style solver with Irons-Tuck acceleration and other optimizations.
- [**FixedEffectModels.jl**](https://github.com/FixedEffects/FixedEffectModels.jl) (Julia), which solves the demeaning problem via LSMR with diagonal preconditioning.
- **PyFixest**, which can run either a MAP solver (analogous to `fixest`'s, without any accelerations) or a conjugate-gradient solver on the within transformation.

All benchmarks are run on Alex' local machine (Mac mini, Apple M4, 10 cores, 16 GB RAM, macOS 15.3.1).

We fit fixed effects regressions using each package's defaults. The Correia datasets are fit with two-way fixed effects, while the fixest DGPs are fit with three-way fixed effects (where the "difficult" DGP is actually difficult). We run each benchmark with and without singleton dropping. We use a tolerance of `1e-8`. Note that convergence criteria differ across implementations, so the tolerance is only a common stopping threshold and does not guarantee identical convergence rules and behavior.

::: {.callout-note}
Benchmark timings for [**reghdfe**](https://scorreia.com/software/reghdfe/) (Stata) can be found on [Sergio Correia's website](https://www.scorreia.com/data/hdfe/index.html). Note, however, that those numbers were collected roughly nine years ago on older hardware, so Python, R, and Julia implementations have a substantial hardware advantage in any direct comparison.
:::

## Reproducing these numbers

To run the benchmarks yourself, you can use the `pixi` tasks below, which will automatically install all required (open source) dependencies, create synthetic data sets and run the benchmarks. You will have to download the Correia datasets from [Correia's website](https://www.scorreia.com/data/hdfe/index.html) and place them in the `benchmarks/modular/data/correia` directory.

```bash
# benchmark fixest-dgp's
pixi run benchmark
# benchmark R, Julia, Python on the Correia datasets
pixi run benchmark-correia
```
